message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier B) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-9). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope examples: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts; on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh for CI). Judge ONLY P9-MO-2.

FINDING P9-MO-2 (low): Bare int()/float() env parsing crashes import with opaque error in seam.py/checkpoint.py
Location: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py + persistence/checkpoint.py (env int/float parsing)
Claim: seam.py and checkpoint.py parse numeric env vars with bare int()/float(), so a malformed value raises an opaque ValueError at import/use with no indication of WHICH env var — the exact class already fixed elsewhere via _int_env (tracing.py, vectorstore.py, per #H-022/#CV-S-1). VERIFY the bare int()/float() calls exist on env values and are not wrapped to name the offending var.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Confirmed bare int()/float() on env vars in both files. Let me check what `_env` does in seam.py and look at the existing `_int_env` helper to confirm the contrast.

content.content:
80	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
81	def _env(name: str, default: str) -> str:
82	    val = os.environ.get(name)
83	    return val if val is not None and val != "" else default
84	
85	
86	def _truthy(name: str) -> bool:
87	    return os.environ.get(name, "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}
88	
89	
90	BULK_MODEL = _env("ASSAY_BULK_MODEL", "llama3.1:8b")
91	BULK_BASE_URL = _env("ASSAY_BULK_BASE_URL", "http://localhost:11434")
92	HIGH_STAKES_MODEL = os.environ.get("ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MODEL") or None
93	
94	BULK_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT", "120")))
95	# Cap BULK generation length. ChatOllama/Ollama default to unbounded (-1); without a cap a
96	# local model can run away to context exhaustion, and the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a
97	# call already blocked in the backend (a leaked pool slot). Bounding output tokens is the real
98	# guard (LangChain's own ChatOllama example sets num_predict explicitly).
99	BULK_NUM_PREDICT = max(1, int(_env("ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT", "2048")))
100	HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT", "300")))
101	
102	MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_REASONING_RETRIES", "2")))
103	BACKOFF_BASE = max(0.0, float(_env("ASSAY_REASONING_BACKOFF", "1.5")))
104	RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF = max(0.0, float(_env("ASSAY_RATELIMIT_BACKOFF", "30")))
105	RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_RATELIMIT_RETRIES", "5")))
106	# run_json re-rolls on a PARSE failure. This is independent of the transient/rate-limit retry
107	# budget above (which applies per generation) so the two do not multiply into a retry storm; an
108	# overall wall-clock deadline bounds the total regardless (#102).
109	JSON_REROLLS = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_JSON_REROLLS", "2")))
110	RUN_JSON_DEADLINE = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_RUN_JSON_DEADLINE", "600")))
111	
112	# OAuth subscription token name (external CLI contract — not renamed).
113	OAUTH_TOKEN_ENV = "CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN"
114

32	def _int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
33	    """Parse an int env var with a clear error naming the var (#CV-S-1, same class as #H-022).
34	
35	    A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` at import time raises an opaque "invalid literal for int()"
36	    that names neither the var nor fires inside any handler.
37	    """
38	    raw = os.environ.get(name, default)
39	    try:
40	        return int(raw)
41	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
42	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc
43	
44	
45	TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006")
46	PROJECT_NAME = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PROJECT", "assay")

55	# Upper bound (milliseconds) on how long the cross-process advisory lock may be waited for
56	# during schema bootstrap. A stuck/crashed peer that still holds a live PG session on the lock
57	# must NOT be able to hang bootstrap forever (#129); lock_timeout aborts the wait. 0 disables
58	# the bound (block indefinitely, the pre-#129 behavior). Read fresh on each bootstrap (#F-033) so
59	# an env var set after import still takes effect; the module constant is the default + the value
60	# tests monkeypatch.
61	_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000")))
62	
63	
64	def _migration_lock_timeout_ms() -> int:
65	    """The advisory-lock wait bound, re-read from the env each call (#F-033).
66	
67	    Falls back to the import-time module constant when the env var is unset, so a test that
68	    monkeypatches ``_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS`` still drives the value.
69	    """
70	    raw = os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS")
71	    if raw is None:
72	        return _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS
73	    return max(0, int(raw))
74	
75	
76	# Brief meta-lock guarding the process-local registries below (atexit registration, the open-pool
77	# list, the per-conn lock map, the pool cache). It is held ONLY for in-memory check-and-set — it
78	# is NEVER held across blocking cross-process I/O (pool.connection(), the advisory-lock wait, or
79	# setup() DDL). That blocking work is serialized per conn_str instead (#129), so a stuck advisory

content.text: The finding is confirmed. Let me reproduce the opaque error to validate the claim.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT=notanint python -c "import assay_engine.reasoning.seam" 2>&1 | tail -5; echo "---CHECKPOINT---"; ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=bad python -c "import assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint" 2>&1 | tail -5
input.description: Reproduce opaque ValueError on malformed env

content.text: Reproduced exactly as claimed. Both files raise an opaque `ValueError: invalid literal for int()` at import time that does NOT name the offending env var, while sibling modules (tracing.py, vectorstore.py) already have the `_int_env` helper that names the var (#H-022/#CV-S-1). This is the same defect class, left unwrapped in seam.py and checkpoint.py.

input.reasoning:
Verified by reading the code and reproducing the failure on branch harden/pass-9.

The bare int()/float() calls on env values exist exactly as claimed and are NOT wrapped to name the offending var:
- src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py uses a `_env(name, default) -> str` helper (lines 81-83) that returns a raw string, then wraps it in bare int()/float(): lines 94, 99, 100, 102, 103, 104, 105, 109, 110.
- src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py uses bare int() on os.environ.get(...) at line 61 (import-time module constant), line 73 (_migration_lock_timeout_ms), and lines 271-273 (pool sizing).

Reproduction confirms an opaque error at import with no var name:
- ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT=notanint python -c "import assay_engine.reasoning.seam" -> `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notanint'` at seam.py:99.
- ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=bad python -c "import assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint" -> `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'bad'` at checkpoint.py:61.

Neither message names the env var, and the traceback points at a generic max(1, int(...)) line — the operator must reverse-engineer which of ~10 env vars in seam.py is malformed.

This is demonstrably the same defect class already fixed elsewhere: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:32-42 and src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py:20 define `_int_env(name, default)` that catches ValueError/TypeError and re-raises `f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}"`, with docstrings explicitly citing #CV-S-1 / #H-022 and describing the exact opaque-import-error problem. seam.py and checkpoint.py were not migrated to that pattern.

Severity (low) is appropriate: it is a misconfiguration diagnosability/operability defect, not a crash on valid input, but it is a genuine, consistency-with-prior-fixes production-readiness issue, not out-of-scope-by-design.

content.text: Verdict delivered: TRUE_POSITIVE for P9-MO-2. The bare int()/float() env parsing exists and is unwrapped in both `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py` (lines 94, 99-110) and `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` (lines 61, 73, 271-273), reproduced as opaque import-time ValueErrors that name no env var — the same class already mitigated via `_int_env` in tracing.py and vectorstore.py.
